exercises.title: A rule is a name, a predicate, and an explanation
exercises.prompt: A rule is a name, a predicate, and an explanation — anything missing one of those three is a vibe. Build a rule structure with all three and an evaluator that, on a violation, returns the rule name and its explanation (not just a boolean). Show a rule passing and a rule violated with its explanation. Discuss why all three parts are required.
exercises.hint: Rule = {name, predicate(response)->bool, explanation}. Evaluate: if predicate fails, return {name, explanation}. Show a pass and a violation with explanation. Three parts: name (to reference), predicate (to check), explanation (so the violation is actionable, not opaque).

exercises.title: Rules in YAML, readable by a non-engineer
exercises.prompt: Rules must be readable by a non-engineer — so they live in YAML (name, condition, explanation) that a policy owner can author and review. Build a YAML-style rule loader that parses declarative rules and compiles them to predicates, and show a non-engineer-authored rule being loaded and enforced. Discuss why declarative rules beat code for policy.
exercises.hint: YAML rule: {name, when: 'response contains code', must: 'end with runnable or assumption', explanation}. Loader parses it -> a predicate. Show a declaratively-authored rule enforced. Declarative: a policy owner (non-engineer) can read/write/review rules without touching code -> policy stays in the policy owner's hands.

exercises.title: Compose rules with precedence and a verdict
exercises.prompt: A response is checked against MANY rules — compose them into a single verdict with precedence (a hard rule overrides a soft one). Build a rules engine that evaluates all rules, collects violations, and produces a verdict respecting rule precedence (hard rule violated -> block; only soft violated -> warn). Show a hard violation blocking and a soft-only violation warning. Discuss why precedence matters.
exercises.hint: Rules tagged hard/soft. Evaluate all; collect violations. Verdict: any hard violation -> block; only soft violations -> warn; none -> pass. Show a hard violation blocking, a soft-only warning. Precedence: not all rules are equal -- a hard safety rule must override a soft style rule.
